title: Autonomous self-burying seed carriers for aerial seeding
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dumr9","text":"Aerial seeding can quickly cover large and physically inaccessible areas to improve soil quality and scavenge residual nitrogen in agriculture, and for postfire reforestation and wildland restoration. However, it suffers from low germination rates, due to the direct exposure of unburied seeds to harsh sunlight, wind and granivorous birds, as well as undesirable air humidity and temperature. Here, inspired by Erodium seeds, we design and fabricate self-drilling seed carriers, turning wood veneer into highly stiff (about 4.9 GPa when dry, and about 1.3 GPa when wet) and hygromorphic bending or coiling actuators with an extremely large bending curvature (1,854 m-1), 45 times larger than the values in the literature. Our three-tailed carrier has an 80% drilling success rate on flat land after two triggering cycles, due to the beneficial resting angle (25°–30°) of its tail anchoring, whereas the natural Erodium seed’s success rate is 0%. Our carriers can carry payloads of various sizes and contents including biofertilizers and plant seeds as large as those of whitebark pine, which are about 11 mm in length and about 72 mg. We compare data from experiments and numerical simulation to elucidate the curvature transformation and actuation mechanisms to guide the design and optimization of the seed carriers. Our system will improve the effectiveness of aerial seeding to relieve agricultural and environmental stresses, and has potential applications in energy harvesting, soft robotics and sustainable buildings.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":667,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ldlgn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"mqp2d","text":"This protocol details the five-step approach to prepare the self-burying seed carriers from wood log. The five-step approach involves the processing of bulk wood into wood veneers with suitable dimensions, the partial removal of lignin and hemicellulose via a short boiling process in an aqueous mixture of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3) to improve the moldability of the wood veneer, followed by three-step mechanical molding including a wet molding, a releasing and a drying step.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":353,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":356,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pz7ns","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

required.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"sxks8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Process lumber to obtain wood veneers (Fig. 1a-h): 3 cm-thick white oak ( Quercus alba ) lumber was acquired from a local lumberyard in North America and prepared in-house. The lumber was flat-sawn into a block with dimensions of roughly 20 cm by 40 cm, with the long edge being parallel to the fiber direction. The block was then sliced into four to five thin sheets, each running through a drum sander to further reduce the thickness until it reached the desired value. The wood veneers obtained after this step had a range of thickness of between 0.4 mm and 0.8 mm. For most of standard three-tailed seed carrier samples, we use 0.4 mm veneers.

Laser-cut wood veneers to obtain flat strip samples: All the test samples and application examples are outlined in the computer-aided design software, Rhinoceros. The 2D outlines (Supplementary Files 1 & 2) were sent to a laser cutter (Universal Laser Systems) and cut from the wood veneer.
Prepare an aqueous solution of 2.5 M NaOH and 0.4 M Na 2 SO 3 in deionized water. Stir to mix well.
Boil the aqueous solution in a crystallizing dish in a fume hood.
Immerse wood veneer strips in the boiling solution for 10 minutes.
Take the wood veneer strips out with glass rods, followed by immersion in boiling deionized water three times to wash the samples and remove the residual chemicals.
Pat dry the wood veneer strips with a paper towel, and place the samples in the open air for about 1 min.
For bending experiment samples:
Hold for 5 seconds, and release the sample from the mandrel.
let the sample dry freely on a non-stick and dry surface.
For coiling samples:
Continue to wrap the wood veneer strip on the mandrel until the whole strip is wrapped.
Hold for 5 seconds, and release the sample from the mandrel.
let the sample dry freely on a non-stick and dry surface.
The three-tailed seed carriers were made with two separately molded tails, which were glued to the end of the active coil (Fig. 1o-q).

In the case of the fully biodegradable seed carrier, the tip was made of a dough by mixing 42 g flour and 112 mL water, shaped by a two-part mold (Fig. 1r-w). After the dough dried completely after 12 h, the tip was dipped into a 20 wt% ethyl cellulose/ethanol solution twice for waterproofing. Immediately after the second dip, the tip was coated with a uniform layer of horse mane hairs, cut into 2 mm fibers by electrostatic flocking.

1. At the step of " Wood veneer processing" , challenges may arise when it comes to the selection of the region where the wood veneer should be cut from. Note that within each annual ring of white oak, the earlywood has larger vessel diameters of ~250 - 500 um than that of latewood at ~20-50 um. Since our sample is quite thin, relative to the thickness of each annual ring (Fig. 2a), we chose only to use the latewood. We believe that excluding the earlywood with relatively large vessels helps decrease the effects of vessel polarity or the distribution on the bending performance. Before cutting, the exact location of the outlines was visually inspected to avoid the earlywood region with large vessel pores with diameters of ~0.25-0.5 mm in the white oak. As shown in Figure 2b, the earlywood regions where large vessels concentrate can be identified at low magnification with the naked eye in the case of white oak. In regions where visually identifiable pores exist, these will be excluded for sample preparation. In addition, to ensure a relatively uniform fiber direction, we laser cut the strip along the wood fiber direction (see Fig. 1g). 2. At the step of " Molding the wood veneer strips" , if we observe the initially straight coiled body bends undesirably, we need to adjust its resting pose after it is released from the mold and during drying. We suggest clipping the tip and ensuring the coiled body stands vertically. 3. At the step of " Molding the wood veneer strips" , if we observe the dried coil is not tight enough, we need to refine the manual coiling process. During the coiling process, we ensured that the edge of the strip was always tightly connected without gaps or overlaps in between. In addition, due to the differences in cellular structures from different wood strips, the total shrinkage ratio between the released and drying stages may vary, which could also result in variability in the pitch.
1. Wood veneer processing: 1 hr 2. Chemical washing: 30 min 3. Molding: 2 min operation and 12 hours drying 4. Tip fabrication: 2 hours

Figure 1. Figure 1.&nbsp;Photos of the fabrication and assembly of the seed carrier prototypes. a-f , The process of turning a white oak log into a wood veneer with a specified thickness and fiber orientation. g-h, The process of laser cutting, chemical washing and manual molding of the wood strips. i , The chemical washing process. j-n , The mechanical molding process. o-q , Adding two additional tails for three-tailed seed carriers. r-w , The tip manufacturing process adapted from the literature 24 with additional seeds embedding. x , The process of molding a double-coiled body. Scale bar is 10 mm unless otherwise specified. Figure 2. Figure 2. a, The relative size and location of a sample sheet situated on a tangential-radial plane of a white oak lumber. The sheet is prepared from the latewood region with relatively small vessels. b , Regions with large vessels are excluded before the laser cutting and strip sample preparation through a visual inspection process relying on human naked eyes.

Supplementary File 1_strip_laser Cut.Dxf Laser cutting file of the strip for the three-tailed seed carrier. Supplementary File 2_tail_laser Cut.Dxf Laser cutting file of the tail for the three-tailed seed carrier. Supplementary File 3_mold_3d Print.Stl 3D Printing file of the mold for the three-tailed seed carrier.
Wrap one pat-dried wood veneer strip on a mandrel.
Wrap one pat-dried wood veneer strip on a 3D printed mold (Supplementary File 3), starting from the tail end. Fix the tail with a rubber band.
